verses.text: And it came to pass in the four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt, in the fourth year of Solomon's reign over Israel, in the month Zif, which is the second month, that he began to build the house of the LORD. And the house which king Solomon built for the LORD, the length thereof was threescore cubits, and the breadth thereof twenty cubits, and the height thereof thirty cubits. And the porch before the temple of the house, twenty cubits was the length thereof, according to the breadth of the house; and ten cubits was the breadth thereof before the house. And for the house he made windows of narrow lights. Guidance: The chronological and architectural opening. (1) 'The four hundred and eightieth year after the children of Israel were come out of the land of Egypt.' This is one of the most important dating notes in the Old Testament. If Solomon's fourth year was around 966 BC (the conventional date), then the Exodus would be around 1446 BC — the 'early date' of the Exodus, accepted by many conservative scholars. Other scholars treat the 480 as a symbolic 12 × 40 (twelve generations of forty years) and prefer a thirteenth-century 'late date' around 1270 BC. Either way, the chapter is locating temple-construction at a specific moment in salvation-history: not the founding of Israel, but the consummation of the wilderness-tabernacle promise. The same LORD who met Moses at Sinai and gave him the tabernacle pattern now sees His permanent house begun in Jerusalem. The Exodus-Sinai-tabernacle-temple sequence is one continuous architectural-theological progress. (2) 'In the fourth year of Solomon's reign… in the month Zif.' Zif is the older Canaanite name for the second month of the Hebrew year (April-May, post-exilic Iyyar). Construction begins in spring. (3) The dimensions: 60 × 20 × 30 cubits (about 90 × 30 × 45 feet, or 27 × 9 × 13.5 meters). Compared to ancient temples elsewhere, the Solomonic temple was not enormous — it was the cella of a Phoenician-style royal chapel, not a public assembly hall. The Israelites worshiped in the courtyards; only priests entered the building itself. (4) The porch (ulam) — 20 cubits wide (matching the building's width) × 10 deep. Like the entrance porch of ancient Syrian and Phoenician temples. (5) 'Windows of narrow lights.' Hebrew chalonei shequfim atumim — 'recessed windows' or 'windows with shutters/grilles' — narrow on the outside, wider on the inside (the standard ancient Near-East defensive-window pattern, letting in light but resisting projectiles). The chapter is beginning with date, dimensions, and detail — the careful precision befitting the building of God's house.

And against the wall of the house he built chambers round about, against the walls of the house round about, both of the temple and of the oracle: and he made chambers round about: The nethermost chamber was five cubits broad, and the middle was six cubits broad, and the third was seven cubits broad: for without in the wall of the house he made narrowed rests round about, that the beams should not be fastened in the walls of the house. And the house, when it was in building, was built of stone made ready before it was brought thither: so that there was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house, while it was in building. The door for the middle chamber was in the right side of the house: and they went up with winding stairs into the middle chamber, and out of the middle into the third. So he built the house, and finished it; and covered the house with beams and boards of cedar. And then he built chambers against all the house, five cubits high: and they rested on the house with timber of cedar. Guidance: Two technical achievements stand out. (1) The side chambers (yatzia) — three stories of rooms built against the outside walls of the temple, running around three sides (the back and two long sides; the porch had no side chambers). Each story was wider than the one below — five, six, seven cubits — because the temple's outer wall stepped back at each story to provide a shelf-rest for the floor beams of the next level up. This is a stroke of structural genius: the temple's main load-bearing wall did not need to be penetrated by floor-beam sockets (which would have weakened it); the beams simply rested on the step-shelves. The side chambers were used for priestly residences, vestments, sacred vessels, and treasury — auxiliary spaces around the holy core. (2) 'The house was built of stone made ready before it was brought thither: so that there was neither hammer nor axe nor any tool of iron heard in the house, while it was in building.' This is one of the most evocative details in the entire chapter. The stones were precisely cut at the quarry; when they reached the Temple Mount, they were assembled in silence. The Talmud and later Jewish tradition saw this as a deeply theological detail. The temple — the house of the God of peace — was built without the sound of iron weapons of war. The quarry, not the building site, took the noise. The principle has been used devotionally for centuries: God prefers His house to be quiet; the noisy preparation happens elsewhere so that the place itself is reverent. The chapter is teaching us that the visible product of holy work is often silent because the labor went into preparation. The temple was a finished thing assembled in worship, not constructed in chaos. The same principle applies, Christians have argued, to the building of the church (1 Pet 2:5) — the LORD shapes living stones quietly elsewhere so that His house rises in worship, not noise.

And the word of the LORD came to Solomon, saying, Concerning this house which thou art in building, if thou wilt walk in my statutes, and execute my judgments, and keep all my commandments to walk in them; then will I perform my word with thee, which I spake unto David thy father: And I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people Israel. Guidance: The mid-construction word — a stunning interruption in the architectural narrative. The LORD speaks to Solomon while the temple is rising. The message has two parts. (1) The condition: 'If thou wilt walk in my statutes, and execute my judgments, and keep all my commandments to walk in them.' Three terms — statutes, judgments, commandments — covering the full vocabulary of Torah faithfulness. The condition is moral fidelity, not architectural completion. (2) The promise: 'I will perform my word with thee, which I spake unto David thy father: and I will dwell among the children of Israel, and will not forsake my people Israel.' Hebrew shakhanti — 'I will tabernacle' — the same root as mishkan (tabernacle) and shekhinah (later rabbinic word for the LORD's manifest presence). The promise is shekhinah-dwelling. The chapter is teaching us a profound principle. The building of the temple is necessary but not sufficient for the LORD's presence. The LORD will not automatically dwell in a building just because the building is built. He dwells where covenant obedience makes Him welcome. A temple without covenant-keeping is just a building; with covenant-keeping, it becomes shekhinah-bearing. The same principle applies to every place that wants to be God's dwelling — the human heart, the gathered church, the family home. Architecture cannot manufacture divine presence; only obedient love invites it. The chapter is structurally placing this word in the middle of the construction-description because the editor wants the reader to understand: the rest of the technical details that follow are meaningless unless covenant fidelity accompanies them. Walls and gold and cherubim are physical containers; the contents depend on Solomon's life.

So Solomon built the house, and finished it. And he built the walls of the house within with boards of cedar, both the floor of the house, and the walls of the cieling: and he covered them on the inside with wood, and covered the floor of the house with planks of fir. And he built twenty cubits on the sides of the house, both the floor and the walls with boards of cedar: he even built them for it within, even for the oracle, even for the most holy place. And the house, that is, the temple before it, was forty cubits long. And the cedar of the house within was carved with knops and open flowers: all was cedar; there was no stone seen. Guidance: The cedar interior. Solomon lines the entire stone interior with cedar — walls, floor (cypress/fir), and ceiling all paneled. 'All was cedar; there was no stone seen.' The visual effect would have been overwhelming: a forest interior of polished cedar with sweet, distinctive cedar fragrance permeating the building. Inside the cedar paneling, the building was divided into two rooms by an interior partition placed 20 cubits from the back wall: the front room (40 × 20 × 30 cubits, called heikhal — 'temple' or 'palace') and the back room (20 × 20 × 20 cubits, called devir — 'oracle' or 'most holy place,' likely related to the root for 'speech' since the LORD spoke from above the ark there). The back room is a cube — 20 × 20 × 20. Cubic shape will appear again in Revelation 21 in the description of the New Jerusalem, which is itself a cube. The Most Holy Place is the architectural prototype of the perfected dwelling of God with His people. The carvings: 'knops and open flowers' (peqa'im u-petzurei tzitzim — possibly gourds/buds and open flowers). The decoration is botanical, like the tabernacle's lampstand. The temple's interior is a garden of carved cedar — a recapitulation of Eden, where God walked with man among the trees. The chapter is showing us a building that subverts the typical ancient-Near-Eastern temple. Pagan temples were dark interiors housing statues of gods; the Israelite temple is a fragrant cedar garden housing the invisible Word of God.

And the oracle he prepared in the house within, to set there the ark of the covenant of the LORD. And the oracle in the forepart was twenty cubits in length, and twenty cubits in breadth, and twenty cubits in the height thereof: and he overlaid it with pure gold; and so covered the altar which was of cedar. So Solomon overlaid the house within with pure gold: and he made a partition by the chains of gold before the oracle; and he overlaid it with gold. And the whole house he overlaid with gold, until he had finished all the house: also the whole altar that was by the oracle he overlaid with gold. Guidance: The purpose and the gold. (1) The purpose of the inner sanctum is explicit: 'to set there the ark of the covenant of the LORD.' Everything in the temple's design serves the ark; the ark is the LORD's footstool, the throne-base where He has agreed to meet His people (Exod 25:22). The temple is, in its deepest design intention, a throne-room for the LORD. (2) The gold overlay. 'He overlaid the house within with pure gold… the whole house he overlaid with gold.' The cedar paneling of the Most Holy Place — already a magnificent material — was further covered with pure gold leaf. The Holy Place was also covered with gold (although the gold may have been applied in lighter patterns rather than as full overlay). Even the cedar altar in front of the oracle was gilded. The gold quantity required would have been immense — 1 Chron 22:14 reports David accumulated 100,000 talents of gold and 1 million talents of silver for the project (numbers possibly hyperbolic, but indicating massive resources). The visual effect: walking from the porch into the holy place would have felt like entering a chamber of luminous gold; passing the partition into the Most Holy Place, even more so. The reflected lamplight from the seven-branched lampstand would have made every surface glow. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is worthy of the most precious materials. The temple was not a frugal place; it was a place of overflowing beauty. The fragrant cedar, the precise dimensions, the gold-clad cherubim — all of it expressed that the One who dwelt here was infinitely valuable. The chapter is showing us a principle: the worship space reflects the worth of the worshipped.

And within the oracle he made two cherubims of olive tree, each ten cubits high. And five cubits was the one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the other wing of the cherub: from the uttermost part of the one wing unto the uttermost part of the other were ten cubits. And the other cherub was ten cubits: both the cherubims were of one measure and one size. The height of the one cherub was ten cubits, and so was it of the other cherub. And he set the cherubims within the inner house: and they stretched forth the wings of the cherubims, so that the wing of the one touched the one wall, and the wing of the other cherub touched the other wall; and their wings touched one another in the midst of the house. And he overlaid the cherubims with gold. Guidance: The two great cherubim — the most distinctive feature of Solomon's Most Holy Place. These are not the same as the smaller cherubim atop the ark itself (Exod 25:18-20); these are massive freestanding statues placed in the Most Holy Place facing east, with the ark beneath their wing-canopy. Their construction: carved from olive wood (etz shemen — literally 'tree of oil,' the oleaster or related tree), each 10 cubits high (15 feet, 4.5 meters). Each cherub had two wings, each 5 cubits long. Standing side by side with wings stretched out: the inner wing of each cherub touched the central point in the middle of the room; the outer wing of each touched the side walls. Total wingspan across both cherubim: 20 cubits — exactly the width of the Most Holy Place. Their function: to form a canopy of wings over the ark, the LORD's earthly throne-platform. The biblical theology of cherubim: they are the throne-bearers and guardians of the LORD's presence (Gen 3:24 — guarding Eden; Ezek 10 — bearing the divine throne-chariot; Ps 18:10 — 'he rode upon a cherub, and did fly'). The two giant cherubim of the Most Holy Place make the room visually a throne-chamber. The LORD is invisible; the empty space between the cherubim, above the ark, is where He is enthroned. The chapter is showing us how Israelite worship handled the paradox of divine presence: there is a throne; there is no statue. The space is shaped to receive the LORD; the LORD himself is not represented by any image. The cherubim are not idols; they are the structural framing of the empty throne where the Invisible chooses to dwell. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's presence is real, locatable, even structured by furniture — but His person is never reducible to image. Both the realism of presence and the rejection of idolatry are preserved together.

And five cubits was the one wing of the cherub, and five cubits the other wing of the cherub: from the uttermost part of the one wing unto the uttermost part of the other were ten cubits. And the other cherub was ten cubits: both the cherubims were of one measure and one size. The height of the one cherub was ten cubits, and so was it of the other cherub. And he set the cherubims within the inner house: and they stretched forth the wings of the cherubims, so that the wing of the one touched the one wall, and the wing of the other cherub touched the other wall; and their wings touched one another in the midst of the house. And he overlaid the cherubims with gold. Guidance: The two great cherubim — the most distinctive feature of Solomon's Most Holy Place. These are not the same as the smaller cherubim atop the ark itself (Exod 25:18-20); these are massive freestanding statues placed in the Most Holy Place facing east, with the ark beneath their wing-canopy. Their construction: carved from olive wood (etz shemen — literally 'tree of oil,' the oleaster or related tree), each 10 cubits high (15 feet, 4.5 meters). Each cherub had two wings, each 5 cubits long. Standing side by side with wings stretched out: the inner wing of each cherub touched the central point in the middle of the room; the outer wing of each touched the side walls. Total wingspan across both cherubim: 20 cubits — exactly the width of the Most Holy Place. Their function: to form a canopy of wings over the ark, the LORD's earthly throne-platform. The biblical theology of cherubim: they are the throne-bearers and guardians of the LORD's presence (Gen 3:24 — guarding Eden; Ezek 10 — bearing the divine throne-chariot; Ps 18:10 — 'he rode upon a cherub, and did fly'). The two giant cherubim of the Most Holy Place make the room visually a throne-chamber. The LORD is invisible; the empty space between the cherubim, above the ark, is where He is enthroned. The chapter is showing us how Israelite worship handled the paradox of divine presence: there is a throne; there is no statue. The space is shaped to receive the LORD; the LORD himself is not represented by any image. The cherubim are not idols; they are the structural framing of the empty throne where the Invisible chooses to dwell. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's presence is real, locatable, even structured by furniture — but His person is never reducible to image. Both the realism of presence and the rejection of idolatry are preserved together.

And he carved all the walls of the house round about with carved figures of cherubims and palm trees and open flowers, within and without. And the floor of the house he overlaid with gold, within and without. Guidance: The wall carvings and the gold floor. The carved scenes on every interior wall: cherubim, palm trees, open flowers. The motifs are precisely Edenic. Cherubim recall the guardians at the gate of the garden in Gen 3:24. Palm trees were a standard symbol of life, victory, and paradise in ancient Near-East iconography. Open flowers represent fertility and beauty. Putting these three together on the temple walls turns the interior into a stylized garden — a re-creation of Eden, where humans walked with God among the trees. The temple is, theologically, a sacred space where the world before the Fall is symbolically present again. To enter the temple is to enter the LORD's garden. 'Within and without' — both walls of the inner partition were carved, so the carvings continued into both rooms. 'The floor of the house he overlaid with gold, within and without' — gold floor for the Most Holy Place and the Holy Place. The whole interior was gold. The chapter is showing us a building where the visible iconography enacts the theological purpose. Every inch of cedar and gold was meaningful — Eden restored, the throne-room of the LORD, the place where heaven and earth touch. The Israelites who saw it through the courts and (for priests) entered it were not in a building; they were in a designed pictorial argument that the LORD dwells with His people in a Paradise the world has lost.

And for the entering of the oracle he made doors of olive tree: the lintel and side posts were a fifth part of the wall. The two doors also were of olive tree; and he carved upon them carvings of cherubims and palm trees and open flowers, and overlaid them with gold, and spread gold upon the cherubims, and upon the palm trees. So also made he for the door of the temple posts of olive tree, a fourth part of the wall. And the two doors were of fir tree: the two leaves of the one door were folding, and the two leaves of the other door were folding. And he carved thereon cherubims and palm trees and open flowers: and covered them with gold fitted upon the carved work. Guidance: The two sets of doors. (1) The door of the oracle (Most Holy Place) — olive wood with olive-wood lintel and door-posts. The lintel and posts comprised one-fifth of the dividing wall (a pentagonal proportion that has interested scholars but is not architecturally specified). The doors were carved with the same iconographic scheme as the walls — cherubim, palm trees, open flowers — and gold-leafed. The Most Holy Place could be entered (only by the high priest, only on the Day of Atonement); the door, when opened, would show even more golden cherubim and palms. (2) The door of the temple (Holy Place, the outer room) — olive-wood posts (one-fourth of the wall) but fir/cypress folding double doors. The bifold design lets the doors open in a tight space. Same carving program: cherubim, palms, flowers, gilded. The detail 'covered them with gold fitted upon the carved work' suggests the gold was applied to the carving in a way that followed the relief — the cherubim and palm trees were gold-on-gold, with the deeper relief differently gilded so the figures stood out from the background. Like a finely-tooled gold panel. The chapter is showing us that the threshold spaces of the temple were as carefully decorated as the walls. The doors themselves were artworks. The chapter is teaching us a principle: even the boundaries between sacred spaces should be beautiful, because the act of crossing into the holy is itself worthy of attention. Doors that the worshiper passes through can be devotional objects, not merely functional thresholds.

And he built the inner court with three rows of hewed stone, and a row of cedar beams. In the fourth year was the foundation of the house of the LORD laid, in the month Zif: And in the eleventh year, in the month Bul, which is the eighth month, was the house finished throughout all the parts thereof, and according to all the fashion of it. So was he seven years in building it. Guidance: The chapter closes with the inner court and the chronological summary. (1) 'The inner court.' Hebrew chatzer ha-penimit — the priests' court immediately surrounding the temple building. The court walls were three courses of cut stone capped by one course of cedar beams — a Phoenician building technique that combined stone strength with timber flexibility (important in earthquake-prone Israel). The 1:3 ratio of cedar to stone repeated throughout the courts and walls; the same technique can be observed in archaeological remains from Megiddo and other Iron Age sites. (2) The dating: foundation laid in Zif (second month) of Solomon's fourth year; completed in Bul (eighth month) of Solomon's eleventh year. 'So was he seven years in building it.' Hebrew sheva shanim — seven years. The number is meaningful. Seven is the number of completion in Hebrew thought (seven days of creation; seven sabbatical cycles to Jubilee). The temple's construction took the time of one perfect cycle. (For comparison, Solomon's own palace would take thirteen years — 1 Kgs 7:1; the king's house was nearly twice as long, a detail that has its own theological significance.) The chapter is ending where it began — with a careful date that locates the project in the precise rhythm of Israelite time. The temple began in the spring of year 4 and finished in the autumn of year 11. Spring planting to autumn harvest — the natural agricultural cycle. The LORD's house rose during seven complete annual cycles of growth and gathering, finished as the year-end harvest was completed. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's projects often take longer than expected and proceed at a rhythm tied to the patient work of seasons. The building is finished. Chapter 7 will turn to the furnishing of the courts. Chapter 8 will bring the ark and the inauguration. The chapter is closing one phase and opening the next.
